explanation: The handouts explain that the ability to be divided into satisfying segments, whether three parts or seven, distinguishes the newspaper feature series from a single magazine article on the same subject. Each new instalment must be read as a one-shot story by the casual reader. Options A, C, and D are not requirements for a series.

question: The handouts note that the modern feature reader is 'tight-scheduled.' How does this affect the modern feature's layout and content?
options: Features are becoming longer and more detailed to provide comprehensive information.
There is a greater emphasis on short pieces, interesting headlines, and pictorial layout.
Features are being removed from newspapers entirely.
The language is becoming more complex to convey more information in fewer words.
explanation: The handouts state that because the reader is running short of time, short and crispy articles are fetching more readership. Emphasis is laid on pictorial layout, quality pictures, and interesting headlines to attract and keep the reader's interest. Options A, C, and D are not the adaptations described.

question: In the Blundell Technique, what is the relationship between the Lead and the Nut Graph?
options: The Lead summarizes the article, and the Nut Graph provides an anecdote.
The Lead provides an anecdotal microcosm, and the Nut Graph explains how that individual case represents a bigger trend or fits into a bigger picture.
The Lead is the conclusion, and the Nut Graph is the introduction.
The Lead and Nut Graph are unrelated and serve different stories.
explanation: The handouts explain that the introduction (Lead) of the Nut Graph illustrates how an individual case is actually representative of a bigger trend or how it fits into a bigger overall picture. This is a well-worn formula central to the Blundell Technique. Options A, C, and D misrepresent this relationship.

question: Blundell's technique for organizing the main body of a feature involves using 'blocks.' What are these blocks based on?
options: They are organized by time, telling the story in chronological order.
They are organized by subject, each representing a different aspect of the main story.
They are organized by the importance of the information, from most to least important.
They are organized randomly to keep the reader guessing.
explanation: The handouts explain that in the Blundell Technique, the body of the story is a series of information blocks. But they're not about time. They're organized by subject. For example, in a story about egg production, one block might be about foreign competition, another about environmental effects, etc. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.

question: According to Bruce DeSilva of The Associated Press, a good ending must do three things. Which of the following is one of those three?
options: Introduce a new, surprising fact.
Nail the central point of the story to the reader's mind.
Ask a question to make the reader think.
Provide a list of sources used in the article.
explanation: The handouts cite Bruce DeSilva, stating a good ending must: tell the reader the story is over; nail the central point of the story to the reader's mind; and resonate, staying with the reader. Option B directly matches the second point. Options A, C, and D are not among the three required functions.

question: The handouts advise writers to 'be a specialist.' What is the primary benefit of specializing in a particular topic?
options: It allows the writer to avoid reading widely about other subjects.
It helps the writer carve a niche, become an expert, and have industry contacts turn to them for information.
It guarantees that every article they write will be published.
It is the only way to make a living as a freelance writer.
explanation: The handouts explain that to carve a niche, you should decide on a topic or area to specialize in. Through prodigious reading and talking to people in the industry, you become an expert. Often, specialty writers know their stuff so well that their industry contacts sometimes turn to them for the latest news and gossip. Options A, C, and D are not the benefits described.

question: The handouts mention the 'iceberg effect' in writing. What does this concept illustrate?
options: That the final story should be very cold and objective.
That a huge amount of research and material lies beneath the surface of a good story, even if it doesn't all make it into the final piece.
That the most important part of the story is the conclusion, like the tip of an iceberg.
That a story should be structured with the most important facts at the top.
explanation: The handouts explain that when you see an iceberg, all you are seeing is the tip. A huge chunk of it is hidden away beneath the surface. Similarly, every good story is derived from a whole bunch of interviews, data, and research material that don't necessarily make it to the final story. Their role is to help you gain a better understanding of the topic. Options A, C, and D are not what the iceberg effect illustrates.

question: What is the advice regarding writing the way you speak?
options: It is a bad habit that leads to informal and unprofessional writing.
It is an effective technique because it makes articles simple to understand and gives them a natural flow.
It should only be used for opinion columns, not for news or features.
It means you should never edit or revise your first draft.
explanation: The handouts advise that when you compose your article, write as if you were speaking to someone. That's the best way to get your message across because it makes your articles simple to understand. Reading your work aloud is a good way to check if it sounds right. Options A, C, and D are contrary to this advice.

question: The handouts suggest two assumptions a writer should make before starting to write. What are they?
options: The reader is an expert and already cares deeply about the topic.
The reader doesn't know anything about the topic and doesn't care either.
The reader has all day to read and wants a detailed analysis.
The reader is only interested in the pictures and will ignore the text.
explanation: The handouts state that before writing, one should assume that the reader doesn't know anything about the topic and that the reader doesn't care either. The first assumption forces the writer to include necessary background, and the second forces the writer to make the story engaging. Options A, C, and D are not the assumptions recommended.

question: According to the handouts, what is the primary source of a columnist's popularity and success?
options: Writing about the most sensational news of the day.
Their efficiency and proficiency to attract and hold the readers.
Being friends with the editor of the newspaper.
Writing columns that are as long as possible.
explanation: The handouts state that the sole criteria for gauging the success of a columnist lie in his efficiency and proficiency to attract and hold the readers. A successful columnist must possess some cardinal points to impel the readers to continue the perusals of the column carefully and with deep and keen interest. Options A, C, and D are not presented as the primary source of success.

question: The handouts note that columnists are often the most criticized of all journalists. Why is this the case?
options: Because they write on the most controversial topics.
Because a column has the longest life of all newspaper items, and people like to keep track of the writers' thinking.
Because they are the least educated among journalists.
Because they are paid more than other writers.
explanation: The handouts explain that a column has the longest life of all newspaper items, as people like to keep track of the writers' thinking. This also adds to the fact that the columnists are the most criticized of all as well. Options A, C, and D are not the reasons given for this phenomenon.

question: The handouts mention that Urdu columnists are more popular among the masses than English columnists. What is one of the primary reasons for this?
options: Urdu columnists are better writers than English columnists.
Urdu newspapers have a much larger, mass-scale readership, while English newspapers have a limited, elite readership.
English columnists only write about politics, while Urdu columnists write about diverse topics.
Urdu newspapers are cheaper than English newspapers.
explanation: The handouts explain that Urdu columnists are more known among the masses primarily because a large scale readership (every literate Pakistani) reads Urdu newspapers. English newspapers do not have widespread readership and have limited influence among the highly educated. Options A, C, and D are not the primary reasons cited.

question: In what way do English columnists typically differ from Urdu columnists in their treatment of popular ideas?
options: English columnists appeal more to emotion and popular sentiment.
English columnists treat ideas through comparisons, contrasts, and arguments, focusing on pragmatism.
English columnists use more anecdotes and personal experiences.
English columnists play up to the gallery more often.
explanation: The handouts state that if the English columnists are treating popular ideas, they are always doing so differently through comparisons, contrasts, and arguments. Pragmatism is the basic line they follow all the time. Options A, C, and D are more characteristic of Urdu columnists.

question: What is a 'Gossip Column' primarily built upon, according to the handouts?
options: In-depth political analysis.
Humorous aspects of life and satire.
Titillating value, often from the film industry.
Advice on how to do things.
explanation: The handouts describe a Gossip Column as one that contains little except its unquestionably titillating value. Its natural habitat is the film magazine, which flourishes on gossip, some malicious, some harmless but unquestionably titillating. Options A, B, and D describe other types of columns.

question: What is the main purpose of a 'Question and Answer Column'?
options: To test the reader's knowledge on current events.
To provide instruction and education by raising questions and answering them in an understandable style.
To allow readers to send in their own jokes and puzzles.
To publish the newspaper's official stance on reader queries.
explanation: The handouts explain that under this style, a columnist gives a question and then answers it. This makes the column easy to understand and provides ample opportunities to raise questions of national importance and then answer them, paving the way for instruction and teaching. Options A, C, and D are not the main purpose.

question: According to the handouts, columns serve as an 'auxiliary to the editorial.' What does this mean?
options: Columns are less important than editorials.
Columns supplement the material on the editorial page by including subjects that may have been excluded or missed.
Columns are always written by the same person who writes the editorials.
Columns are a replacement for editorials.
explanation: The handouts state that though columns comprise diverse subjects, they also include those topics which incidentally or otherwise have been excluded or missed in the editorial page. In this way, columns are actually the supplements to the editorials and prove the best auxiliaries to the editorial articles. Options A, C, and D are not accurate.

question: What is the 'Anecdotal Style' of column writing?
options: A style where a single subject is discussed in detail throughout the column.
A style where a number of unrelated and related stories and observations are gathered in a single column, separated by asterisks.
A style where the column is divided into different departments like 'In the mailbox'.
A style where the columnist presents a mass of unrelated facts separated by periods.
explanation: The handouts describe the Anecdotal Style as one in which a number of unrelated and related stories and a variety of observations are gathered in a single column. These unrelated stories and varied observations bear no similarities and are separated from each other by asterisks or other typographical devices. Options A, C, and D describe the Unified, Departmental, and Unrelated-facts styles, respectively.

question: In the structure of a column, what is the role of the 'nut graph' or 'angle'?
options: To provide the crux of the matter in the lead.
To give a slant to the idea to get the reader involved, determined by local conditions and the writer's efficiency.
To sum up the column and raise or answer questions.
To provide a collection of unrelated facts.
explanation: The handouts explain that the nut graph or angle is usually determined by local conditions and the predilections of the concerned writers. Any 'Angling' must consider the reader's intent and understanding. It gives a slant to the idea to get the reader involved. Options A, C, and D describe the lead, conclusion, and a content type, respectively.

question: The handouts advise columnists to 'maintain your focus.' What is the result of not following this advice?
options: The column will be too short.
The message will be muddled, and it will be difficult to make a strong impression on the reader.
The column will be more interesting and varied.
The editor will be more likely to publish it.
explanation: The handouts state that you should make your column about one thing and one thing alone and not muddle the message. That's the only way to make a strong impression on your readers and to convince them that your point of view is correct. Options A, C, and D are not the results of losing focus.

question: Why is it important for a columnist to 'be critical' and name names, according to the handouts?
options: It is the only way to get people to read the column.
It creates a bit of controversy, which makes the column an interesting and exciting read, as long as it's not libelous.
It guarantees that the columnist will become famous.
It is a legal requirement for all political columns.
explanation: The handouts advise that people like reading columnists who dare to criticize real-life people, not just nameless concepts. Naming names might create a bit of controversy but as long as you do not libel anyone and don't go overboard, it works well to make your column interesting and exciting. Options A, C, and D are not the reasons given.

question: According to the handouts, what is one of the easiest ways for a columnist to find a new topic or subject?
options: Waiting for inspiration to strike.
Discussing news items from daily newspapers.
Copying the topic from a foreign columnist.
Asking the editor to assign a topic.
explanation: The handouts state that one of the easiest ways to find an idea is by discussing news items. This also helps in actualizing the dictum 'Bring to focus what is current.' Options A, C, and D are not presented as the easiest or recommended methods.

question: The handouts emphasize that a columnist's subject must be 'close to your heart.' What is the reasoning behind this?
options: It makes the writing process faster.
It ensures the subject is something that makes you think and want to share, making it less likely to be a trite or routine subject.
It guarantees that readers will be interested in it.
It is a rule set by the editor.
explanation: The handouts explain that a subject must be touching your heart or mind, and it can even be an incident from ordinary life. Anything that makes you think, and you want to share, can be a subject for a column, but it must never be a trite or routine subject. Options A, C, and D are not the reasoning provided.

question: What does the handouts identify as a key professional quality for a columnist regarding knowledge of laws and ethics?
options: Knowing the laws is optional as long as the writing is good.
A responsible columnist knows the laws, keeps journalistic ethics in mind, and never relegates the editorial policy to redundancy.
Only the newspaper's editor needs to know the laws, not the columnist.
Ethics are a personal matter and do not affect the columnist's professional work.
explanation: The handouts state that huge responsibilities are on a columnist's shoulders. A responsible columnist knows the laws, keeps journalistic ethics in mind, and never relegates the editorial policy to redundancy. He also knows the readers and respects their religious feelings and nationalistic sentiments. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.

question: The handouts advise columnists to 'avoid hyper mannerism.' What is the recommended balance?
options: To follow all rules strictly and never deviate.
To follow the rules, but violate them sometimes to enhance interest, writing like a friend talking but communicating like an intellectual.
To ignore all rules and write in a completely unique, individual style.
To always use the most complex and unusual language possible.
explanation: The handouts suggest that mannerism is a must, but a columnist should avoid hyper mannerism. He must follow the rules, but violate them sometimes to enhance interest. He should write as if talking to a friend, while communicating like an intellectual discussing an important subject. Options A, C, and D are not the recommended balance.
topic: What must be practised by a columnist?

question: For a 'Reporting-in-Depth Column,' what is a key source of material besides the writer's own relationship with decision-makers?
options: Gossip from the film industry.
Constant reading and never leaving the track of things.
Letters from readers asking for advice.
Personal dreams and fantasies.
explanation: The handouts explain that for Reporting-in-Depth Columns, the writer's own relationship with people who are the decision-makers brings a lot of material. Besides this, he has to read constantly, while never leaving the track of things. Options A, C, and D are sources for other types of columns, like gossip, Q&A, or personal reflection.

question: The handouts mention 'carry-over transitional devices' as a useful writing technique. What is an example of such a device?
options: Starting a new paragraph with 'First, second, third...'
Forging a link between the first sentence of a new paragraph and the last sentence of the preceding paragraph.
Using the word 'said' for all attributions.
Ending every paragraph with a question.
explanation: The handouts explain that carry-over transitional devices involve forging a link between the first sentence of your new paragraph and the last sentence of the preceding paragraph. Examples given include: 'His books depart from tradition in another way...' and 'Perhaps so, but DEA officials say...'. Options A, C, and D are not examples of this specific device.

question: What is the advice regarding the use of 'exclamation points' in writing?
options: Use them frequently to add excitement to the copy.
They are a sign of an amateur and should be used very rarely, if at all.
They are essential for writing humorous columns.
They should be used at the end of every sentence in the lead.
explanation: The handouts advise that unless you're writing for a market that likes exclamation points, you might as well never use them. Frequent use will mark you as the greenest of amateurs. You can't punch up a dull piece of copy by applying an exclamation point to it. Options A, C, and D are contrary to this advice.

question: The handouts warn against 'switching tenses.' What is the recommended practice?
options: Use past tense for some sentences and present for others to add variety.
Pick one tense (past or present) and stick with it consistently throughout the story.
Always use future tense for predictions and past tense for facts.
Tenses can be switched randomly without affecting the reader's understanding.
explanation: The handouts explain that attributions should not hop back and forth. You should pick one tense and stick with it. Consistency is the hallmark of a good writer. Options A, C, and D are not recommended practices.

question: What does the handouts advise a writer to do if they are confused about something after writing a column?
options: Ignore the confusion and hope the reader won't notice.
Talk to an expert or consult the source. Never send confusion-ridden writing to the editor.
Add more words to try to clarify the confusion.
Publish it anyway and issue a correction later.
explanation: The handouts explicitly state that if the confusion persists even after writing, talk to the expert of the subject. Never send confusion-ridden writing to the editor. Options A, C, and D are not recommended and go against the advice to ensure clarity and accuracy.

question: According to the handouts, what is the main difference between an article and a feature?
options: An article discusses a subject from one prominent angle, while a feature discusses it from all possible angles.
An article is always longer than a feature.
An article is personalized, while a feature is objective.
An article is only found in magazines, while a feature is only found in newspapers.
explanation: The handouts clearly state that in an article, the writer is not discussing a subject from all possible angles and is sticking to an aspect only, which is totally unlike a feature. Options B, C, and D are not accurate distinctions. Features can be long, features can have personalized elements (subjectivity), and both can appear in newspapers and magazines.

question: The handouts mention 'solicited' and 'unsolicited' articles. What is the difference?
options: Solicited articles are written by staffers, while unsolicited are by freelancers.
Solicited articles are requested by the editor with guidelines, while unsolicited are submitted on the writer's own initiative.
Solicited articles are paid, while unsolicited are published for free.
Solicited articles are about current events, while unsolicited are about timeless topics.
explanation: The handouts explain that both novices and experienced writers contribute articles on their own, and sometimes they are requested to write, which are named as 'Solicited' articles. For solicited articles, guidelines are given. Unsolicited articles are those submitted without a prior request. Options A, C, and D are not the defining difference.

question: What is the main reason for a writer to use 'corroboration' in their research?
options: To make the article longer and more detailed.
To add proof or certainty to an account with new information, objectively demonstrating the honesty of a source.
To ensure the information is from a primary source.
To make the article more entertaining for the reader.
explanation: The handouts define corroboration as obtaining information from a separate, also believable source that substantiates the first source's fact. This objectively demonstrates, if not the accuracy, at least the honesty of your source. Options A, C, and D are not the primary purpose of corroboration.

question: Which type of article reveals the innermost feelings of a person in a confidential tone, often without using their name?
options: Utility Articles
Personality Sketch Articles
Confession Articles
Opinionated Articles
explanation: The handouts define Confession Articles as those that reveal innermost feelings of a person in a confidential tone, while the intimate experiences or of others; people are uninterested in publishing their name. The 'Inside story' of the unknown is to entertain, reform, or inform. Options A, B, and D are different types of articles.

question: In the context of writing an article, what is a 'mind map' used for?
options: To create a visual outline and overcome writer's block.
To map out the geographic locations mentioned in the article.
To record the psychological state of the interview subject.
To create a diagram of the article's target audience.
explanation: The handouts describe mind-mapping as a technique to overcome writer's block. You visualize your subject as the hub, with arrows as spokes leading to the points you will make. This creates a visual outline for your article. Options B, C, and D are not what mind-mapping is used for in this context.

question: According to the handouts, what is one of the most common mistakes article writers make when they are tired?
options: Writing too many long sentences.
Misspelled words and poor grammar.
Using too many quotes.
Writing about topics that are too narrow.
explanation: The handouts list several common mistakes, stating that when writers are tired, they are prone to misspelled words and poor grammar. These are the top two common article-writing mistakes. Options A, C, and D are not the specific mistakes highlighted as most common when tired.

question: The handouts advise against 'plagiarising or 'buying articles'.' What is the recommended approach instead?
options: It is acceptable to copy ideas as long as you change a few words.
Be original and let the words flow from your mind into your article.
Buying articles is acceptable if you get a good deal.
Paraphrasing is the best way to avoid plagiarism.
explanation: The handouts advise to be original and let the words flow from your mind into your article. Paraphrasing can also be classified as plagiarism. Buying articles is not a great idea, especially without an exclusive license. Options A, C, and D are not recommended.

question: What is the function of the 'topic sentence' in an article?
options: To provide an interesting anecdote that hooks the reader.
To let readers know early just what you plan to talk about in your article.
To conclude the article and leave a lasting impression.
To list all the sources used in the article.
explanation: The handouts explain that it is important to let readers know early just what you plan to talk about in your article so they do not expect something more or something different. The place to announce your topic is in your topic sentence, which always comes right after the lead. Options A, C, and D describe the lead, conclusion, and references, respectively.

question: In the context of writing an article, what does it mean to 'harkens back to the lead' in the conclusion?
options: To summarize the lead in a single sentence.
To make the conclusion have at least some reference, even if implicit, to the lead to give the story unity.
To repeat the lead word-for-word at the end.
To place the lead again at the end of the article.
explanation: The handouts advise that the conclusion must not be divorced from the Lead. It must have at least some reference to the Lead, even though an implicit one. Explicit reference is not a bad idea either, but it should be connected to the lead to give the story unity. Options A, C, and D are not accurate descriptions of 'harkening back'.
topic: What to do when you have written the article?

question: What is a 'Roundup Article'?
options: An article that collects and summarizes all the news from the past week.
An article that sums up an activity of any nature, such as seminars, conferences, or festivals.
An article that predicts future trends and events.
An article that provides a biography of a famous person.
explanation: The handouts define Roundup Articles as those where activity of any nature is summed up. Seminars, conferences, festivals, etc., are covered for magazines in this format. They are not difficult to write if the writer has been attending the functions. Options A, C, and D are not the definition of a roundup article.

question: The handouts state that 'history is its context' for journalism. What does this imply for article writing?
options: Articles should only be written about historical events.
Articles about historical events are written, but are mostly connected to current ones, letting history flow with the current.
Journalists must have a degree in history.
The context of an article is less important than the facts.
explanation: The handouts explain that journalism is current, but history is its context, and the thumb rule is 'Let history flow with the current'. Articles about historical events are written, but are mostly connected to the current ones, and historical events are never written in isolation. Options A, C, and D are not accurate interpretations.

question: In the landmark U.S. Supreme Court case The New York Times v. Sullivan, what new standard for libel was established for public officials?
options: They must prove the statement was false and defamatory.
They must prove the statement was published with 'actual malice'.
They do not need to prove any damages.
They are not protected by libel laws at all.
explanation: The handouts explain that in The New York Times v. Sullivan, the Supreme Court said that a public official could not maintain a suit for damage to reputation unless the official could prove that the libellous statement was published with 'actual malice' (knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for the truth). Options A, C, and D are not the standard established by this case.
topic: Legal and ethical considerations for writers

question: What does 'actual malice' mean in the context of libel law, as defined by the Supreme Court?
options: Hatred or ill will towards the subject.
The writer either knew the statement was false or published it with reckless disregard for whether it was false.
The writer intended to cause emotional distress.
The statement was published in a competing newspaper.
explanation: The handouts specify that 'actual malice' has nothing to do with the dictionary definition of malice. It means that the writer or publisher either knew that the statement was false or went ahead and published it with a reckless disregard for whether or not it was false. Options A, C, and D are not the legal definition.
topic: Legal and ethical considerations for writers

question: Which of the four divisions of invasion of privacy involves taking a factual situation and embellishing, fictionalizing, or distorting it?
options: Revealing intimate details of someone's life.
Misappropriating someone's name or likeness.
Casting someone in a false light.
Intruding physically into someone's private life.
explanation: The handouts explain that casting someone in a false light can happen by embellishing a factual situation, fictionalizing it, or distorting it. This is one of the four separate divisions of the tort of invasion of privacy. Options A, B, and D are the other three divisions.
topic: Legal and ethical considerations for writers

question: Under copyright law, if a writer's work is considered 'work made for hire,' who holds the copyright?
options: The individual writer.
The employer (e.g., the newspaper).
It becomes public domain.
The copyright is shared between the writer and the employer.
explanation: The handouts state that under the current law, if your writing efforts are 'work made for hire,' then the copyright falls to the employer. This means a newspaper reporter has no individual protection for stories written in the line of duty. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.
topic: Legal and ethical considerations for writers

question: What does the legal doctrine of 'fair use' allow a writer to do?
options: Use any copyrighted material without permission as long as credit is given.
Copy a portion of another writer's words without permission, so long as it is done within reason.
Use entire books for research purposes.
Publish any document that is over 50 years old.
explanation: The handouts explain the doctrine of 'fair use' as allowing you to copy a portion of another writer's words without the author's permission, so long as you do so within reason. The problem is determining how much is within reason, and there are no hard-and-fast rules. Options A, C, and D are not accurate descriptions of fair use.
topic: Legal and ethical considerations for writers

question: What is the recommended ethical stance for a writer when a source says, 'This is off-the-record,' after revealing information?
options: It's acceptable to use the information since you heard it.
If you listened without objection, you are morally committed to keep it off-the-record.
Off-the-record agreements are not binding for journalists.
You can use the information but not the source's name.
explanation: The handouts explain that if your source places something off-the-record and you listen to it without objection, then you are morally committed to keep it off-the-record. The recommended procedure is to interrupt immediately and say you'd rather not hear it. Options A, C, and D are not the recommended ethical stance.
topic: Legal and ethical considerations for writers

question: According to the handouts, which scholar noted that language shapes thoughts and emotions, determining one's perception of reality?
options: John Stuart Mill
Edward Sapir
Benjamin Whorf
Noam Chomsky
explanation: The handouts specifically attribute to Benjamin Whorf the idea that language shapes thoughts and emotions, determining one's perception of reality. John Stuart Mill is quoted as saying 'Language is the light of the mind.' Edward Sapir's ideas are about language as a fundamental expression of social identity. Noam Chomsky is not mentioned in this context.

question: The handouts mention that Pakistan is blessed with virtually two official languages. Which languages are these?
options: Punjabi and Sindhi
Urdu and English
Urdu and Arabic
English and Pashto
explanation: The handouts state that in Pakistan, we are blessed with virtually two official languages flourishing in a multicultural society. These are English, one of the international languages, and Urdu, which is given the label of being 'Official'. The other options are regional languages or languages of religious significance, but not the two de facto official languages mentioned.

question: In feature writing, what does the term 'subjectivity' allow a reporter to do?
options: Report only the objective facts without any personal bias.
Inject his own emotions and thoughts into the story, sometimes using 'I' and 'we'.
Write the story from multiple, neutral perspectives.
Ensure the story is based on verified data and statistics.
explanation: The handouts explain that some feature stories are written in the first person using 'I' and 'we', thus allowing the reporter to inject his own emotions and thoughts. This inclusion makes the feature story subjective. Options A, C, and D describe objectivity, which is the opposite of subjectivity.

question: The handouts state that features 'usually strike the keynote in the opening sentences.' What is the purpose of this?
options: To provide a comprehensive summary of the entire story.
To allow the readers to come into quick contact with the crux of the story and arouse interest.
To present the least important information first.
To confuse the reader and build suspense.
explanation: The handouts explain that features usually strike the keynote in the opening sentences, which permits the reader to come into quick contact with the crux of the story and arouses interest. This is different from a news lead, which summarizes the story. Options A, C, and D are not the purpose of striking the keynote.

question: A feature is described as a 'complete portrayal of the subject.' What does this require from the writer?
options: Writing the story from only one perspective.
Covering all possible aspects from all possible angles.
Ensuring the feature is exactly 1000 words long.
Focusing solely on the positive aspects of the subject.
explanation: The handouts explicitly clarify that 'complete portrayal' means covering all possible aspects of the subject from all possible angles. This ensures a thorough and balanced presentation. Options A and D are the opposite of a complete portrayal. Option B is incorrect as length is not part of the definition of completeness.

question: According to the handouts, what is a key difference between a news story and a feature in terms of deadline pressure?
options: News stories have no deadlines, while features must be written quickly.
A news story is hurriedly written, while a feature writer seldom has to meet a steep deadline.
Both are written under the same strict deadline pressure.
Features have stricter deadlines than news stories.
explanation: The handouts explicitly state that a news story is hurriedly written while a feature story seldom has to meet a steep deadline. Since deadline pressure is not as intense as for a daily reporter, a feature writer can take sufficient time to write and rewrite to achieve the best quality. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.

question: The handouts discuss 'commemorative features.' Which of the following is an example of a topic for such a feature?
options: The invention of a new smartphone
The daily commute of a factory worker
Eid Milad-un-Nabi or Quaid-i-Azam Day
A review of a new restaurant
explanation: The handouts list commemorative articles as those based on national and important international events, fairs, exhibits, and personalities. Examples given include Eid Milad-un-Nabi and national heroes' days like Quaid-i-Azam Day. Options A, B, and D are examples of other source categories like science, daily life, and reviews.

question: What is a key source of material for 'psychological features' according to the handouts?
options: Scientific journals and textbooks.
The constant stresses and strains of modern life and resulting social problems.
Biographies of famous scientists.
Travelogues and personal diaries.
explanation: The handouts state that there have been numerous psychological problems due to the constant stresses and strains of modern life. This has created a need for features that address issues like class consciousness, marital instability, and mental complexities. Options A, C, and D are not the primary sources mentioned for psychological features.

question: When evaluating a website's 'accuracy,' what should a journalist primarily look for?
options: Whether the website has a lot of images and graphics.
Whether the website's URL is short and easy to remember.
Whether the website has a high traffic rank on Alexa.
Whether the data and information can be verified for correctness.
explanation: The handouts' website checklist includes 'Accuracy' and notes that many a time there are glaring mistakes. A journalist must verify the data and information. Options A, B, and C are not part of this specific checklist item for evaluating accuracy.

question: According to the handouts, what is the purpose of 'truncating back' a URL when evaluating a web page?
options: To make the URL shorter for citation purposes.
To delete the end characters of the URL to find the publisher or more information about the author/site.
To count the number of sub-directories in the web address.
To check if the URL contains the word 'article' or 'feature'.
explanation: The handouts describe the technique of 'truncating back' a URL. This involves deleting the end characters of the URL, stopping just before each slash, to see if you can find more about the author or the origins/nature of the site providing the page. Options A, C, and D are not the described technique.

question: A good feature writer should possess a 'keen sense of observation.' What does this enable them to do?
options: Write very quickly without needing to research.
Detect interesting points that usually escape the eyes of a common man.
Memorize large amounts of information.
Predict future events with accuracy.
explanation: The handouts state that a cardinal characteristic of a good feature is the keen sense of observation to detect those interesting points, which usually escape the eyes of a common man. Without this, the feature cannot find matters of common interest. Options A, C, and D are not what keen observation enables.

question: The handouts suggest that features should sometimes take up 'light articles.' What is the purpose of this?
options: To fill space in the newspaper when there is no hard news.
To match the generally depressing contents of the newspapers and provide amusement and entertainment.
To avoid writing about serious topics.
To appeal only to young readers.
explanation: The handouts explain that sometimes feature writers take up light articles in order to match the generally depressing contents of the newspapers. News stories are commonly impersonal and unattractive, so lightly written articles provide amusement and entertainment. Options A, C, and D are not the purpose stated.

question: What is a key demand of a feature regarding the presentation of an issue?
options: To present only one side of the picture to avoid confusion.
To give and explicate both sides of the picture to reflect real life.
To present the issue in a way that supports the newspaper's editorial policy.
To avoid any kind of criticism or comments.
explanation: The handouts state that a feature shall be considered consummate and perfect only if it gives and explicates both sides of the picture of an issue, an event, or a problem. This is because a feature reflects real life and should provide a balanced view. Options A, C, and D do not reflect this core principle.

question: The handouts mention that features 'render valuable social services.' How do they achieve this?
options: By ignoring social problems and focusing on entertainment.
By examining countless social and psychological problems and suggesting possible solutions.
By promoting the government's policies.
By publishing letters to the editor.
explanation: The handouts explain that features render valuable social services by examining the countless social and psychological problems and their suggestive and possible solutions. They impel readers to ponder over serious topics. Options A, C, and D are not how features render social services.

question: In the context of interviews, what is the primary disadvantage of a 'group session' interview?
options: It is too time-consuming to arrange.
It is not a valid form of interview for journalistic purposes.
Everyone in the room may want to answer, and answers may be tainted by the presence of others.
It is impossible to take notes during a group session.
explanation: The handouts state that the only advantage to the group interview is that it's better than no interview at all. Its disadvantages are that everyone in the room may want to answer your question, or the subject's answers may be tainted by the presence of a PR representative. Options A, B, and D are not the primary disadvantages mentioned.

question: Why should a writer research the interviewee before the interview?
options: To find embarrassing information to use against them.
To be able to spend the interview time more effectively and flatter the subject with your thoroughness.
To decide whether the interviewee is worth talking to.
To write the story before the interview takes place.
explanation: The handouts explain that if you enter an interview armed with detailed information about your interviewee, you'll be able to spend the interview time more effectively and you'll also be likely to flatter the subject with your thoroughness. Options A, C, and D are not the reasons given.

question: What is an 'open-ended question' in the context of an interview?
options: A question that can only be answered with a 'yes' or 'no'.
A question that allows the respondent a broad range in the answer.
A question designed to antagonize the interviewee.
A question that is not written down beforehand.
explanation: The handouts define an open-ended question as one that allows the respondent broad range in the answer. It warns, however, that if the question is unfocused, the answer is useless. Option A describes a yes-or-no question. Option C describes a loaded question. Option D is not a type of question.

question: According to the handouts, what is a 'probe question' used for?
options: To start the interview and break the ice.
To ask for amplification of an unresponsive or incomplete answer.
To end the interview on a positive note.
To ask for the interviewee's personal opinion.
explanation: The handouts define the probe question as one that asks for amplification of an unresponsive or incomplete answer. For example, 'Why do you recommend Prof (name) basic course?' Options A, C, and D are not the purpose of a probe question.

question: A 'Description Intro' in a feature story is used under what condition?
options: When the event being described is not very important.
When a comparatively few words can formulate a vivid image in the reader's mind.
When the writer wants to summarize the entire story.
When the story is about a historical event.
explanation: The handouts define the Description Intro as consisting of a description of either a person, place, or event. It is used when a comparatively few words can formulate a vivid image in the reader's mind. Options A, C, and D are not the conditions for its use.

question: What is a 'Suspended Interest Intro'?
options: An intro that gives away the ending of the story.
An intro, usually several sentences, where the reader's interest is intensified as they continue to read, used for news-features.
An intro that is very short and to the point.
An intro that uses a direct quote from the subject.
explanation: The handouts define the Suspended Interest Intro as one that usually consists of several sentences in which reader's interest is intensified as he continues to read. It is used most frequently for news-feature or feature stories which must be printed in their entirety to preserve their news interest. Options A, C, and D do not define this type.

question: In the structure of a feature, what is the role of the 'conclusion'?
options: To attract immediate attention and pull the reader into the story.
To provide a solid transition from the lead into the body.
To give the reader a sense of satisfaction and tie the conclusion to the lead for unity.
To present the most important facts first.
explanation: The handouts explain that the conclusion should give the reader a sense of satisfaction. You need to tie the conclusion to the lead so that the story has unity. This can be done through a short, tight summary, an anecdote, or a quote. Options A, B, and D describe the lead, transition, and news structure, respectively.

question: The handouts advise that in the main body, one should 'never fill it, important part.' What is the risk of trying to 'fill' the body with unnecessary material?
options: The article will become too long for the editor.
The reader might leave it even in the middle of the feature if they feel bored or over-burdened.
The writer will run out of things to say.
The article will not meet the word count.
explanation: The handouts caution that people can try to fill the part thinking the reader is gripped, but it is never the case. Newspaper reading is a willing activity, and if the reader feels bored or over-burdened, they might leave it even in the middle. Options A, C, and D are not the primary risk mentioned.

question: The handouts mention that pictures can be 'blown up' in size. Under what condition is this acceptable?
options: Any picture can be blown up to any size.
Pictures can be blown up if their pixels stay intact.
Only black and white pictures can be blown up.
Pictures should never be blown up as it distorts the image.
explanation: The handouts state that pictures can be blown in size, if their pixels stay intact. This ensures the image quality is maintained. Options A, C, and D are not the conditions mentioned.
topic: Selection of Pictures, Illustrations and Maps

question: What is a 'collage' in the context of picture usage in newspapers?
options: A single, large picture that takes up a whole page.
An amalgamation of pictures arranged in a specific manner.
A picture with a long, descriptive caption.
A black and white version of a color picture.
explanation: The handouts define a collage as an amalgamation of pictures arranged in a specific manner. It is a favourite of quite a few, but these must be used sporadically. Options A, C, and D are not the definition of a collage.
topic: Selection of Pictures, Illustrations and Maps

question: Why is it important for a writer to 'know what you can do best'?
options: To be able to write on any subject without any effort.
To focus on subjects that suit their nature, rather than attempting areas where they are likely to fail.
To prove that they are better than other writers.
To get a job at the most popular newspaper.
explanation: The handouts state it is a must for the writer to know on what he can write best. If he can write on social issues better, he must not attempt political subjects, as it can be against his nature and contribute to his failure. Options A, C, and D are not the reasons given.

question: The handouts advise that a feature should have a 'constructive approach.' What does this entail?
options: Focusing only on positive news and ignoring problems.
Including elements of reformatory approach, criticism, and suggestions for accountability.
Avoiding any kind of criticism to stay on good terms with everyone.
Supporting the government's policies unconditionally.
explanation: The handouts explain that features include elements of constructive and reformatory approach, criticism, commentary, hard facts, and suggestions. Features point out corrupt elements and subject them to some extent of accountability. Options A, C, and D are not part of a constructive approach.

question: The handouts describe a type of feature called the 'Personality Column.' What is its main characteristic?
options: It is written by a famous personality.
The writer filters the news through the mesh of their own personality to give it an individual touch.
It is a biography of a famous person.
It contains only gossip about celebrities.
explanation: The handouts explain that some feature writers take up another type of feature called the 'Personality Column', where they filter the news through the mesh of their own personalities to give it an individual touch. Options A, C, and D are not the definition of this type.

question: What is a key difference between a news feature and a timeless feature?
options: A news feature is always longer than a timeless feature.
A news feature is tied to a breaking news event, while a timeless feature does not have to be used immediately.
A news feature is about people, while a timeless feature is about things.
A news feature is written in the first person, while a timeless feature is not.
explanation: The handouts define a news feature as one that is usually tied to a breaking news event and is often written under deadline pressure. A timeless story, on the other hand, does not have to be used immediately and can be written leisurely over a longer period. Options A, C, and D are not accurate distinctions.

question: The handouts mention that a feature story can be a 'sidebar.' What is a sidebar?
options: A short, humorous story placed in the margin of the page.
A 'side' article that accompanies a main news story.
A summary of the main news story.
An opinion piece written by the editor.
explanation: The handouts explain that a news feature is called a 'sidebar,' in that it is a 'side' article that accompanies the main news story. It is a type of feature that is tied to a breaking news event. Options A, C, and D are not the definition of a sidebar.

question: The handouts mention that a feature writer for a magazine has 'more room to play.' What does this refer to?
options: The ability to play sports while on assignment.
More freedom with ideas, words, space, and time compared to daily newspaper writing.
A larger office and more comfortable working conditions.
The opportunity to travel more for stories.
explanation: The handouts explain that journalists feel more comfortable while writing for a magazine primarily because of the allowed word limit, more space, and long deadlines. They have more room to play with ideas, words, and space besides having more time at their expense. Options A, C, and D are not what 'room to play' refers to.

question: How does a feature's 'shelf life' compare to a daily newspaper's?
options: Dailies have a longer shelf life than magazines.
Magazines have a longer shelf life, and features can become part of history as people keep them.
Both have the same shelf life.
Neither has any shelf life beyond the day of publication.
explanation: The handouts state that dailies have a short shelf life in comparison to magazines, whereas magazines have a longer shelf life. Magazine features also become part of history, as people keep them in their book racks. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.

question: What is the primary purpose of a 'profile' story?
options: To provide a detailed list of a person's accomplishments.
To paint a word portrait of the subject, enabling readers to see and get to know them.
To interview a person about a specific event.
To promote the subject's latest project.
explanation: The handouts explain that a true profile, whether short or long, must enable readers to see and get to know the subject. This is achieved through research, observation, and skilful interviewing, not just by listing facts. Options A, C, and D are too narrow or miss the essence of a profile.

question: According to the handouts, what is a key requirement for a successful feature series?
options: It must be written in a single day.
Each new instalment must be readable as a one-shot story for the casual reader.
All instalments must be published on the same day.
It must focus on a single, insignificant topic.
explanation: The handouts state that the series demands that each new instalment be read as a one-shot story by the casual reader. It must have an attention-getting lead and a solid, preferably suspenseful ending to hook the reader for the next instalment. Options A, C, and D are not requirements for a series.

question: The handouts discuss the 'Blundell Technique' for modern feature writing. What is the first element of this technique?
options: The Nut Graph
The Lead
The Main Body
The Conclusion
explanation: The Blundell Technique has four parts: 1. The Lead (Intro), 2. Nut Graph (Angle), 3. Main Body (Blocks), and 4. Conclusion (Ending). The Lead is the first element, typically an interesting anecdote that serves as a microcosm of the bigger story. Options A, C, and D are the subsequent elements.

question: In the Blundell Technique, what is the function of the 'Nut Graph'?
options: To provide a short, snappy sentence that acts as the lead.
To explain the entire article in a nutshell and illustrate how the lead's anecdote fits into a bigger trend.
To present all the main facts in a summary form.
To serve as the conclusion of the story.
explanation: The handouts define the Nut Graph as a paragraph that explains your entire article in a nutshell. It shows how the individual case in the lead is representative of a bigger trend or fits into a bigger overall picture. It is considered the single most important bit of writing. Options A, C, and D describe other parts of the article.

question: According to Blundell, what are the six key blocks that can be used to organize the main body of a feature?
options: Introduction, Rising Action, Climax, Falling Action, Resolution, Moral
History, Scope, Cause, Impact, Action of contrary forces, The future
Who, What, When, Where, Why, How
Problem, Solution, Argument, Counter-argument, Example, Conclusion
explanation: The handouts list Blundell's six key blocks: History (background), Scope (extent of the problem), Cause (why it's happening), Impact (who and what is affected), Action of contrary forces (who is doing what), and The future (how it will be). Not all need to be included, and they don't have to appear in this order. Options A, C, and D are different organizational structures.

question: The handouts advise writers to 'be your own harshest critic.' What does this entail?
options: Deleting all your work and starting over every time.
Constantly reviewing your work, trying to find a more compelling lead, a stronger angle, and more colourful quotes.
Only focusing on the negative aspects of your writing.
Asking everyone you know to criticize your work.
explanation: The handouts explain that good writers are rarely satisfied. They constantly review their work, role-playing the reader, and are always trying to improve it. This is what it means to be your own harshest critic. Options A, C, and D are not the meaning of this advice.

question: What is the 'iceberg effect' in writing, as mentioned in the handouts?
options: That the most important information should be at the top of the story.
That a good story is derived from a huge amount of research and material, most of which doesn't make it into the final piece.
That the writer's emotions should be hidden beneath the surface of the story.
That the story should be cold and objective.
explanation: The handouts explain that when you see an iceberg, all you are seeing is the tip. A huge chunk of it is hidden away beneath the surface. Similarly, every good story is derived from a whole bunch of interviews, data, and research material that don't necessarily make it to the final story. Their role is to help you gain a better understanding of the topic. Options A, C, and D are not what the iceberg effect illustrates.

question: The handouts provide three rules of thumb for writing tightly. One of them is: 'If it's possible to cut a word out, cut it out.' What is the underlying goal of this rule?
options: To make the article as short as possible, regardless of content.
To ensure the copy is concise, punchy, and more interesting to read.
To make the article easier for the editor to cut.
To save the newspaper money on printing costs.
explanation: The handouts explain that tight copy consumes fewer column inches, and it's punchier and more interesting to read. The goal is to be concise and avoid unnecessary words. Options A, C, and D are not the primary goals of this advice.

question: What is the definition of a column according to Prof Shafique Jhallandhari?
options: A long, detailed article on a single subject.
All those writings published on regular basis under a permanent caption in the periodicals.
A short, factual news report.
A letter from a reader published in the newspaper.
explanation: The handouts cite Prof Shafique Jhallandhari's definition: 'All those writings are columns published on regular basis under a permanent caption in the periodicals and the periodicals.' Options A, C, and D are not this definition.

question: Why are columns considered the 'most powerful medium of any newspaper, next to news'?
options: Because they are the longest items in the paper.
Because their message is very strong, they are read by decision-makers, and columnists have a loyal following.
Because they are the easiest type of writing to produce.
Because they are always written by the newspaper's editor.
explanation: The handouts explain that columns' message is always very strong, both for readers and for those connected to the writing. They are read by decision-makers and their critics. Columnists become known over time, and readers follow them, making them a powerful tool. Options A, C, and D are not accurate reasons for their power.

question: The handouts mention that Urdu columnists have a much wider readership than English columnists. What is the primary reason for this?
options: Urdu columnists are better writers than English columnists.
Urdu newspapers have a mass-scale readership, reaching every literate person, while English newspapers have a limited, elite readership.
English columnists only write about politics, while Urdu columnists write about diverse topics.
Urdu newspapers are cheaper than English newspapers.
explanation: The handouts explain that Urdu columnists are more known among the masses primarily because a large scale readership (every literate Pakistani) reads Urdu newspapers. English newspapers do not have widespread readership and have limited influence among the highly educated. Options A, C, and D are not the primary reasons cited.

question: According to the handouts, how do English columnists typically differ from Urdu columnists in their approach to writing?
options: English columnists appeal more to emotion and popular sentiment.
English columnists are more pragmatic, using comparisons, contrasts, and arguments.
English columnists use more anecdotes and personal experiences.
English columnists play up to the gallery more often.
explanation: The handouts state that if the English columnists are treating popular ideas, they are always doing so differently through comparisons, contrasts, and arguments. Pragmatism is the basic line they follow all the time. Options A, C, and D are more characteristic of Urdu columnists.

question: What is an 'Essay Column' as described in the handouts?
options: A column that provides advice on how to do things.
A column that arouses nostalgia and mood, with an unlimited range but must never be deductive or dull.
A column that answers questions from readers.
A column that covers sports events.
explanation: The handouts describe the Essay Column as one that arouses a similar nostalgia and mood in his writing. It has style, an unlimited range, but it must stick to one rigid rule—it must never be deductive or dull. Options A, C, and D describe other types of columns.

question: What is the main purpose of a 'How-to-do or Advice Column'?
options: To entertain readers with humorous stories.
To educate readers with gentle instruction that does not appear to be a lesson.
To provide the latest news and gossip.
To criticize government policies.
explanation: The handouts explain that How-to-do or Advice Columns educate the readers, as there is a gentle instruction written in such a way as not to appear to be a lesson. These columns usually appear on magazine and daily pages. Options A, C, and D are not the main purpose of this type.

question: One of the objectives of column writing is to create a 'friendly atmosphere.' How does the column achieve this?
options: By using very formal and complex language.
By creating a direct, personal contact with readers without any intermediary.
By avoiding any discussion of current affairs.
By publishing the column only once a year.
explanation: The handouts state that it is the objective of column-writing to make a friendly and amicable atmosphere, without any intermediary, through direct contact with the columnist. The readers feel a newness, freshness, and friendliness in the writing. Options A, C, and D are contrary to this objective.

question: Which style of column writing involves dividing the material into different departments, such as 'in the mailbox' or 'passing parade'?
options: Unified Style
Anecdotal Style
Departmental Style
Unrelated-facts-style
explanation: The handouts describe the Departmental Style as one where a columnist has arranged and managed material that can be easily divided and separated into different departments. This greatly assists in turning each department into an interest-absorbing one. Options A, B, and D describe other styles.

question: In the structure of a column, what is the role of the 'conclusion'?
options: To bring into focus what the columnist wants to say.
To give a slant to the idea to get the reader involved.
To sum up the column, raising or answering questions, and leaving the reader satisfied.
To present a collection of unrelated facts and news items.
explanation: The handouts explain that the conclusion is for summing up the column, where one must be raising the questions or answering the questions previously raised. The success is that the reader must feel satisfied after reading. Options A, B, and D describe the lead, nut graph, and a content type, respectively.

question: The handouts advise columnists to 'refer to facts.' Why is this important?
options: It makes the column longer and more detailed.
Arguments, however logical, will not carry much weight unless accompanied by facts that support the position.
It shows the columnist has done a lot of research.
It is a requirement set by the editor.
explanation: The handouts explain that your arguments, however logical, will not carry much weight unless they are accompanied by facts that support your position. Don't overdo it, but do make use of facts from reputable sources. Options A, C, and D are not the primary reason given.

question: What is the advice regarding 'providing a solution' in a column?
options: It is not necessary to provide a solution; just raising the issue is enough.
Columns that criticize policies but offer no solutions are useless; a good columnist suggests a solution.
Only the government can provide solutions, not a columnist.
Providing a solution is only for advice columns, not for political ones.
explanation: The handouts advise to not just raise an issue but to have the conviction to suggest a solution. Columns that criticize certain policies but offer no solutions are useless. People read columns because they want to gain insight and answers. Options A, C, and D are contrary to this advice.

question: The handouts suggest that a columnist should 'choose from the news items of dailies' for topics. What is the benefit of this approach?
options: It guarantees the topic will be popular.
It helps in actualizing the dictum 'Bring to focus what is current'.
It is the easiest way to avoid doing any original research.
It ensures the topic is not controversial.
explanation: The handouts state that one of the easiest ways to find an idea is by discussing news items. This also helps in actualizing the dictum 'Bring to focus what is current,' as news items cover current ideas. Options A, C, and D are not the benefits mentioned.

question: The handouts warn that a columnist must never choose a subject based on 'rumour.' What is the correct approach?
options: Rumours are acceptable as long as they are interesting.
A good columnist never targets someone on mere hearsay and confirms whatever is being said before writing.
Rumours can be used if they are from a reliable source.
It is acceptable to use rumours if the columnist does not name the source.
explanation: The handouts advise that gossip is usually less brainy and true, and one must think about it. A good columnist never targets someone on mere hearsay, and he confirms the 'news' and 'views' before writing about it. Options A, C, and D are not recommended.

question: The handouts identify 'sense of humour' as a key quality for a columnist. What caution is given regarding its use?
options: It should be used in every column to keep it light.
The columnist must never use gross language or dirty jokes.
Humour should only be used in sports columns.
It is not an important quality for a columnist.
explanation: The handouts state that humour makes a columnist popular, but it is a must for him that he never uses gross language or dirty jokes. Some columnists use jokes but to good avail. Options A, C, and D are not the caution given.

question: What does the handouts identify as a key professional quality for a columnist regarding their knowledge?
options: It is not important to be well-read; only writing skill matters.
Reading of almost all subjects is a necessity, and one subject must be known thoroughly.
A columnist only needs to know about politics.
Knowledge can be faked by quoting other sources.
explanation: The handouts state that reading is a must for a columnist. Reading of almost all subjects is a necessity because knowing everything around him and mastering one can do. One subject, he must know it thoroughly because he acts as a preacher-cum-opinion maker. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.

question: The handouts advise columnists to 'avoid hyper mannerism.' What is the recommended approach to style?
options: To develop a unique and rigid style that never changes.
To follow the rules, but violate them sometimes to enhance interest, like talking to a friend but communicating as an intellectual.
To copy the style of a famous columnist.
To avoid any kind of personal style altogether.
explanation: The handouts suggest that mannerism is a must, but a columnist should avoid hyper mannerism. He must follow the rules, but violate them sometimes to enhance interest. He should write as if talking to a friend, while communicating like an intellectual discussing an important subject. Options A, C, and D are not the recommended balance.
topic: What must be practised by a columnist?

question: For an 'Opinionated Column,' where does the primary source of material come from?
options: Interviews with experts and officials.
The columnist's own thought, idea, extensive training, and long-time observations.
Letters from readers.
News reports from other newspapers.
explanation: The handouts explain that for reflective-type or opinionated columns, the material is not so difficult to gather. The columnist acts as an expert based on extensive training and long-time observations, and then expounds their opinions for the general public. Options A, C, and D are sources for other types of columns.

question: The handouts mention 'using analogies' as a useful writing device. What is an analogy?
options: A comparison of similarities.
A figure of speech that uses 'like' or 'as'.
A short, humorous story.
A direct quote from an expert.
explanation: The handouts define an analogy as a comparison of similarities. They note that like figures of speech, bad analogies can get you in trouble, but good analogies are effective. Options B, C, and D are not the definition of an analogy, though a simile (B) is a type of analogy.

question: What is the advice regarding the use of 'direct address' (using 'you') in writing?
options: It should be avoided as it is too informal.
It is a standard device for involving the audience and pulling them into the story.
It should only be used in opinion columns.
It is considered unprofessional and should never be used.
explanation: The handouts state that direct address is a standard device for involving your audience. It reaches out to readers and pulls them into the story, making them active participants rather than passive observers. Options A, C, and D are contrary to this advice.

question: The handouts list 'jargon' as a common writing problem. Why should writers avoid jargon?
options: Because it shows the writer is not an expert.
Because it makes sense only to people who work in specific professions and makes readers work too hard to understand.
Because it is always grammatically incorrect.
Because it is not allowed by newspaper style guides.
explanation: The handouts explain that jargon consists of phrases that crop up within various professions and make sense only to people who work in those professions. Writers should avoid it because it makes readers work overtime to understand. Options A, C, and D are not the primary reason for avoiding jargon.

question: The handouts warn against 'mind-reading' in writing. What does this term refer to?
options: Trying to guess what the reader is thinking.
Using word constructions like 'The mayor feels that...' instead of 'The mayor says...'.
Imagining a scene in your head and describing it.
Predicting future events in the story.
explanation: The handouts advise against using phrases like 'The mayor feels that...' and suggest using 'The mayor says...' instead. This is called mind-reading and has no place in professional reporting. The same goes for describing emotions; show the emotion, don't tell the reader what someone felt. Options A, C, and D are not the definition of this problem.

question: The handouts provide several tips for what to do after writing a column. Why is it important to 're-check facts'?
options: Because the editor will not check them.
Because nothing should be wrong there, and it's not safe to assume it's correct just because you checked it once.
To make the article longer by adding more details.
To ensure the article meets the word count.
explanation: The handouts advise to re-check facts, as nothing should be wrong there. Do not take it for granted that it will be corrected as you have already checked it. There could be a lot of mistakes. Options A, C, and D are not the reasons given for re-checking facts.

question: What is the advice regarding 'feedback' on a published column?
options: Ignore all feedback; the writer knows best.
Feedback should be evaluated as something of utmost importance, and one should never be less considerate towards the observations.
Only positive feedback should be considered.
Only feedback from the editor is important.
explanation: The handouts state that feedback should be evaluated as something of utmost importance, and one never be less considering towards the observations. Listen and read carefully, as these make you a better writer. Options A, C, and D are not the recommended approach.

question: According to the handouts, what is the main area for article writing?
options: Daily newspapers
Magazines
Television broadcasts
Radio programs
explanation: The handouts state that the magazine is the main area for articles, though Op-Ed pages are also finding space for articles. Options A, C, and D are not identified as the main area.

question: What does the handouts identify as a primary reason why article writing is considered a 'first step towards better writings'?
options: It is the easiest and requires no skill.
It helps novices learn about subject treatment, structural handling, and playing with ideas before moving to other genres.
It guarantees publication.
It is the only way to get paid as a writer.
explanation: The handouts explain that mostly it is advised by others to start writing articles, and mending their ways of writing besides imparting training to novices in the art of writing. Later, they move to other genres, like features and columns, as experience is gained through article writing. Magazines act as a net-practice arena. Options A, C, and D are not the reasons given.

question: When researching for an article, what is the key principle regarding the quantity of information?
options: Collect only as much information as you think you will use.
Always collect more information than you think you are going to use, so you can choose the best and most apropos.
It is better to have too little information than too much.
The amount of information is not important; the writing style is what matters.
explanation: The handouts state that there should always be a rule to collect always more information than we think we are going to use; so you choose the best and the most apropos in making the points. Options A, C, and D are contrary to this advice.

question: What is 'plausibility' as a reliability check for research facts?
options: The fact is from a primary source.
The fact makes sense to you and presumably to other people, subjectively.
The source of the fact has a proven track record.
The fact can be confirmed by another source.
explanation: The handouts define plausibility as concluding that, subjectively, the fact makes sense to you and presumably to other people. That is how you will write it – not as verified fact but as plausible consideration. Options A, C, and D describe authenticity, credibility, and corroboration, respectively.

question: Which type of article is based on recognized authorities' opinions or ideas, giving prominence to the concept and ideas of the person?
options: Utility Articles
Personal Experience Articles
Interview Articles
Narrative Articles
explanation: The handouts define Interview Articles as those based on recognized authorities or famous persons' opinion or ideas in which prominence is given to the concept and ideas of the person, while considering what he is important for the readers. Options A, B, and D are other distinct types of articles.

question: What is a 'Narrative Article'?
options: An article that tells a story in the first person.
An article that presents facts through devices like a short story, using conversational and descriptive style.
An article that provides a step-by-step guide.
An article that reveals the writer's innermost feelings.
explanation: The handouts define Narrative Articles as those that present facts through devices, like a short story. It is in a conversational and description style, which is used to heighten effect and entertain as well as inform the readers. Options A, C, and D describe other article types.

question: The handouts advise using 'key words' in the title of an article. What is the purpose of this?
options: To make the title look more professional.
To attract readers and search engines.
To summarize the article in a few words.
To ensure the article is taken seriously.
explanation: The handouts advise using words in your title that attract readers and search engines. These words are known as keywords. Doing a search can help select which keywords to use. Options A, C, and D are not the primary purpose of using keywords.

question: The handouts mention 'your resource box is as important as your article.' What is a resource box?
options: A box at the beginning of the article that lists the sources used.
A short author bio and advertisement at the end of the article where you can promote yourself or your website.
A box containing definitions of key terms used in the article.
A box with links to related articles.
explanation: The handouts explain that the resource box is where you get your two seconds of advertising. Make it fun and interesting to compel readers to click to your website for more. It is the pay-off for giving your article up for reprints. Options A, C, and D are not what a resource box is.

question: One of the common article-writing mistakes is 'too much hype, bragging and self-promotion.' Where should any selling or pitching occur?
options: In the headline of the article.
Throughout the body of the article to keep the reader engaged.
Only in the RESOURCE BOX below the body of the article.
It should never occur at all.
explanation: The handouts advise that it is better to only sell or pitch your company in the RESOURCE BOX below the body of the article. Research indicates that the resource box gets a good click-through rate. Options A, B, and D are not the recommended placement.

question: The handouts warn against articles that are 'vague or generalised.' What is the recommended approach?
options: To be straight forward and to the point, and not 'beat about the bush'.
To use very complex language to sound authoritative.
To cover as many topics as possible in one article.
To keep the article very short, regardless of clarity.
explanation: The handouts advise to be careful not to 'beat about the bush.' Say what you mean, and mean what you say. Options A, C, and D are not the recommended approach to avoid vagueness.

question: In the four elements of a good article, what is the function of 'the lead or the intro'?
options: To let readers know early what you plan to talk about.
To be the bait that hooks the reader into reading on into the story.
To bring in anecdotes, quotes, and exposition.
To leave readers feeling that the article has come to a successful conclusion.
explanation: The handouts explain that the lead is built into the very first one or more paragraphs and is the bait that hooks the reader into reading on into the story. A good lead is the best selling tool. Options A, C, and D describe the topic sentence, main body, and ending, respectively.

question: In an article, what is the difference between the 'focus' and the 'slant'?
options: Focus and slant are the same thing.
Focus is the writer's point of view, and slant is the objective parameter.
Focus is the objective parameter, and slant is the writer's point of view, revealed between the lines.
